project_description: Restore hydrologic function and habitat on the 1,654-acre McCormack-Williamson Tract in a manner that also helps provide flood relief to the North Delta region by reconnecting the site as a floodplain to the Mokelumne River.
target_species: Chinook salmon; Steelhead trout; Delta smelt

project_description: Habitat restoration effort in southern Sacramento County, near the confluence of the Cosumnes and Mokelumne Rivers, that will restore over 200 acres of floodplain and wetland habitats to support native fish and wildlife through hydrologic reconnection and native plant enhancement.
target_species: Chinook salmon; Steelhead trout; Delta smelt

project_description: 7 miles of setback levees in the Lower Elkhorn Basin along the east side of the Yolo Bypass.
project_type: bypass floodplain habitat; fish passage improvement
target_species: Chinook salmon; Steelhead trout; Green sturgeon; White sturgeon

project_description: Restoration of 176 acres of floodplain habitat by removing rock revetment, recontouring the riverbank, and converting agricultural land back to native riparian habitats, enhancing fish and wildlife habitat, floodplain function, and public recreation opportunities, while also reducing flood management costs.
Enhance floodplain habitat through improved connections to the Feather River and the Sacramento River.
Modify operations to create seasonal floodplain rearing habitat in the Tisdale and Sutter Bypasses.
Remove a fish passage impediment and install fish-protective screens in the Yolo Bypass.
funding_sources: WCB grant; USBR grant
project_type: fish screen installation or improvement; fish passage improvement
target_species: Chinook salmon; Steelhead trout; Green sturgeon; White sturgeon

project_name: Prospect Island Tidal Habitat Restoration Project
project_description: Restore daily tidal inundation across a diked historical marsh on Prospect Island (located within the Cache Slough Complex in Solano County) by breaching levees to reinstate tidal flow and enhance habitat for native fish, improve water quality, bolster climate resilience, and sequester carbon.
target_species: Chinook salmon; Steelhead trout; Delta smelt
display_name: Prospect Island Tidal Habitat Restoration Project

project_description: Transformation of the Little Egbert Tract, a below-sea-level agricultural area at the southeastern end of the Yolo Bypass near Rio Vista, into enhanced habitat and flood conveyance zones by breaching existing levees, constructing subtidal swales and berms, and restoring native riparian and tidal ecosystems while also improving recreational opportunities and flood protection for nearby communities.
target_species: Chinook salmon; Steelhead trout; Green sturgeon; White sturgeon

project_description: Restoration of approximately 2,200 acres at the downstream end of the Yolo Bypass in Yolo County to create a connected network of tidal marsh, floodplain, and wildlife-friendly agricultural habitatsbalancing flood management, ecosystem enhancement, and continued land use.
target_species: Chinook salmon; Steelhead trout; Delta smelt

project_description: Converts approximately 1,200 acres of former grazing land near Oakley in the western SacramentoSan Joaquin Delta into a restored tidal marsh, re-establishing daily tidal flow to support native fish and wildlife, improve flood resilience, and serve as a living laboratory and community-accessed park.
construction_completion_year_comments: Phase 1 completed 2021, now in Phase 2 (scheduled for completion in 2028).
target_species: Chinook salmon; Steelhead trout; Delta smelt

project_name: P68 Feather River Salmon Habitat Improvement Project
project_description: In 2023, the first phase of the P68 Feather River Salmon Habitat Improvement Project added approximately 8,300 cubic yards of optimal sized salmon spawning gravel at four locations in the Feather River between RM 63 to 67 to increase the amount of suitable and accessible spawning habitat for salmonids in the Feather River. Gravel will be added at other locations upstream of the locations that received gravel in 2023 when the second phase of the project is completed in 2026.
funding_sources: Prop 68; CVPIA; DWR

project_name: Oroville Wildlife Area River Restoration Project at Robinson's Riffle
project_description: The Oroville Wildlife Area River Restoration (OWA-RR) Project at Robinson’s Riffle is a river channel and floodplain enhancement project focused on improving salmon habitat within the OWA. The project involves lowering the riverbank to generate gravel material, which will be added to the riverbed so adult fish can spawn, as well as creating side channels and floodplain habitat for juvenile fish. The project also has recreation, flood and erosion risk reduction, and aesthetic benefits.
construction_completion_year_comments: The improvements are anticipated to be completed in October 2027. Demobilization, site clean-up, and construction closeout is anticipated to be completed in December 2027. Shortly after, the post-project monitoring efforts will begin.
estimated_budget_comments: The estimated total project cost is the cost associated with the construction phase of the project. This includes time and material costs for construction implementation as well as the following services: project management, environmental and monitoring, engineering services during construction, construction management, and post-project monitoring.
funding_sources: Wildlife Conservation Board Greenhouse Gas Reduction Fund
target_species: Chinook salmon; Pacific lamprey; White sturgeon; Green sturgeon; Steelhead trout
display_name: Oroville Wildlife Area River Restoration Project at Robinson's Riffle
